title: I Wanna Be Around

jokes.text: Oh, is the coffee that bad this morning?
jokes.setup: Tony is violently pounding on the coffee machine
jokes.punchline: Character assumes he's reacting to bad coffee taste rather than mechanical failure
jokes.explanation: Simple misdirection joke with good economy - assumes wrong cause for Tony's frustration

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: This coffee is terrible!

dialogue.speaker: Unknown character
dialogue.line: Yeah, that'll happen when you beat it to death like that.

jokes.text: Which of the machines has that?
jokes.setup: Someone suggests getting a screwdriver to fix the coffee machine
jokes.punchline: Jim thinks screwdriver is a drink and wonders which vending machine has it
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment showing Jim's oblivious nature - could only be delivered by this type of character

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: A screwdriver? Which vending machine has that?

jokes.text: And I'm not supposed to tell why no matter what.
jokes.setup: Character is told not to reveal why the tool room is locked
jokes.punchline: Immediately reveals the secret in the very next line
jokes.explanation: Classic setup/payoff showing character's inability to keep secrets

dialogue.speaker: Character
dialogue.line: Don't worry, I won't tell anyone why the tool room is locked.

dialogue.speaker: Character
dialogue.line: It's because Bobby set the socket wrench collection on fire.

jokes.text: I wish he'd put it up to a vote.
jokes.setup: Discussion about Louie's determination to survive the apocalypse
jokes.punchline: Deadpan wish that Louie's survival could be voted on (implying they'd vote against it)
jokes.explanation: Dark humor delivered with perfect deadpan economy - genuinely subversive

dialogue.speaker: Unknown character
dialogue.line: I wish your survival could be voted on.

jokes.text: You get tired of salami.
jokes.setup: Someone questions why Louie is eating wheat from a barrel
jokes.punchline: Louie treats eating raw wheat as a normal lunch alternative to salami
jokes.explanation: Louie's matter-of-fact delivery makes the absurd seem reasonable

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: What's wrong with wheat? It's got fiber, it's got carbs. You want I should eat salami all day? This is healthier!

jokes.text: Judas!
jokes.setup: Louie discovers someone revealed his secret food storage
jokes.punchline: Dramatically calls the revealer 'Judas' - biblical betrayal over survival supplies
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment - Louie's tendency toward dramatic overreaction in a single word

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Judas! You're Judas!

jokes.text: You know that special twinkle he's got in his eye? Yeah. Well, it was gone, as gone as your brain.
jokes.setup: Louie describes how even fearless Donahue was scared by the doomsday expert
jokes.punchline: Compares the missing twinkle to the listener's missing brain
jokes.explanation: Excellent structure - builds up serious point then pivots to classic Louie insult

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Even Donahue was scared. You know what scared him most? The guy said when the end comes, there won't be a twinkle in the sky. No twinkle.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Donahue said, 'What do you mean no twinkle?' The guy said, 'No twinkle. Just darkness.' Donahue turned white as a ghost.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: I said, 'Donahue, what are you worried about? You ain't never had a twinkle in the sky.' He said, 'Yeah, but at least I knew it was there.'

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: You know what? You're just like Donahue. You ain't got a twinkle neither. But then again, you ain't got much of a brain either.

jokes.text: Oh, boo hoo.
jokes.setup: Louie lists apocalyptic fears including nuclear holocaust and class warfare
jokes.punchline: Dismissive 'boo hoo' response to Louie's litany of doom
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy - two words deflate Louie's entire dramatic monologue

dialogue.speaker: Louie De Palma
dialogue.line: Nuclear holocaust, class warfare, overpopulation...

dialogue.speaker: Unknown character
dialogue.line: Boo hoo.

jokes.text: Well, thanks, Confucius.
jokes.setup: Someone gives Louie simple advice about taking good with bad
jokes.punchline: Louie sarcastically calls them Confucius
jokes.explanation: Standard sarcastic comeback - fits Louie's character but not particularly original

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Oh, sure, Confucius!

jokes.text: Welcome to Louie's Doom Room.
jokes.setup: Louie shows off his survival shelter
jokes.punchline: Calls it 'Doom Room' instead of something positive like 'Safe Room'
jokes.explanation: Perfect character naming - Louie's pessimistic worldview in two words

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: I call it my Doom Room.

dialogue.speaker: Other Character
dialogue.line: Doom Room? Why not call it a Safe Room or something positive?

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Because that's what it is—a room for when doom comes.

jokes.text: not these foreigners they got now.
jokes.setup: Louie praises the Irish and Italian laborers who built the garage
jokes.punchline: Calls modern workers 'foreigners' despite praising immigrants himself
jokes.explanation: Perfect character irony - Louie's selective prejudices revealed through unconscious hypocrisy

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: These Irish and Italian laborers, they built this garage with their own two hands. Hardworking people, you know? Real Americans.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: But nowadays? All these foreigners coming in, taking jobs. It's not the same anymore.

jokes.text: What good is money in the bank, Nardo, when you're a radioactive pretzel?
jokes.setup: Nardo comments on the cost of Louie's survival supplies
jokes.punchline: Louie's vivid image of being a 'radioactive pretzel'
jokes.explanation: Genuinely original and vivid imagery - 'radioactive pretzel' is both absurd and memorable

dialogue.speaker: Nardo
dialogue.line: Louie, do you know how much you spent on survival supplies?

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: How much?

dialogue.speaker: Nardo
dialogue.line: Three hundred dollars!

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: So? When that nuclear bomb goes off, I'm gonna be the only one left. And you know what I'm gonna be? A radioactive pretzel.

jokes.text: I need him to convince the black hordes I'm cool.
jokes.setup: Louie explains why Jeff is on his survival squad
jokes.punchline: Reveals racist assumptions about post-apocalyptic social dynamics
jokes.explanation: Character-revealing moment showing Louie's casual racism and strategic thinking

jokes.text: I'll do what I can.
jokes.setup: Louie's racist comment about needing Jeff to convince 'black hordes'
jokes.punchline: Jeff's dry, resigned acceptance of this absurd role
jokes.explanation: Perfect deadpan response - Jeff's weary acceptance of Louie's absurdity shows great character dynamics

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: Yeah, sure, Louie. I'll just tell them, 'Hey, fellas, Mr. De Palma says it's okay if you don't rob the garage tonight.'

jokes.text: When you're up to your pretty, dimpled cheeks in rubble
jokes.setup: Louie tries to convince Nardo she'll need his shelter
jokes.punchline: His creepy focus on her 'pretty, dimpled cheeks' while describing disaster
jokes.explanation: Perfectly creepy Louie moment - mixing apocalyptic imagery with inappropriate leering

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: You're gonna need me, Nardo. When the big one hits, you're gonna need shelter. And I got a nice little place in the basement.

dialogue.speaker: Nardo
dialogue.line: Louie, I can take care of myself.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Oh sure, sure. But when the earthquakes come and the buildings are crashing down all around you, you're gonna wish you had a nice, safe place. A place with strong walls. Where you could stay close to me. Real close. Just you and me in that basement, your pretty, dimpled cheeks pressed up against...

dialogue.speaker: Nardo
dialogue.line: Louie!

jokes.text: That's disgusting! But memorable.
jokes.setup: Louie gives Nardo a disgusting password, she calls it disgusting
jokes.punchline: Louie admits it's disgusting but defends it as memorable
jokes.explanation: Good structure - Louie's practical defense of his inappropriate behavior

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Yeah, it's disgusting. But you're not gonna forget it.

jokes.text: If you and I were the last man and woman on Earth, we'd be the last man and woman on Earth.
jokes.setup: Implicit romantic suggestion from Louie about being alone together
jokes.punchline: Nardo's circular logic emphasizing nothing would happen between them
jokes.explanation: Clever circular construction that emphasizes the futility while being quotable

dialogue.speaker: Nardo
dialogue.line: Nothing would happen.

dialogue.speaker: Nardo
dialogue.line: Because nothing ever happens between us.

dialogue.speaker: Nardo
dialogue.line: And nothing ever will happen between us.

dialogue.speaker: Nardo
dialogue.line: Because we're not that kind of people.

dialogue.speaker: Nardo
dialogue.line: And even if we were that kind of people, nothing would still happen.

dialogue.speaker: Nardo
dialogue.line: Because nothing ever does.

jokes.text: Yes, Iggy, I was thinking of you... the last time I stepped on a snail in my bare feet.
jokes.setup: Iggy asks if Louie was thinking of him for the survival squad
jokes.punchline: Louie compares thinking of Iggy to the disgusting experience of stepping on a snail
jokes.explanation: Perfect Louie insult - specific, visceral imagery with great timing and structure

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Thinking of you? That's like stepping on a snail.

jokes.text: Trust me, Iggy, you'll never know the difference.
jokes.setup: Iggy says he doesn't want to die
jokes.punchline: Louie implies Iggy is so oblivious he wouldn't notice being dead
jokes.explanation: Dark humor that perfectly captures both characters - Louie's cruelty and Iggy's obliviousness

dialogue.speaker: Iggy
dialogue.line: I don't want to die.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Don't worry, you won't notice.

jokes.text: But it wouldn't work, because he'd vie with me for leadership of the group. Besides, he'd use up too much oxygen.
jokes.setup: Discussion of why Alex wouldn't work for the survival squad
jokes.punchline: Escalates from leadership concerns to petty oxygen consumption complaint
jokes.explanation: Good escalation showing Louie's petty concerns mixed with practical ones

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Alex, you can't work for the survival squad. You don't have what it takes to be a leader.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: What do you mean? I'm perfectly capable of leading people.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: That's not the point. The real problem is you breathe too much oxygen.

jokes.text: Someone with a lot of muscle, and a single-digit I.Q.
jokes.setup: Louie describes his ideal enforcer for the survival squad
jokes.punchline: Combination of 'muscle and single-digit IQ' clearly describing Tony
jokes.explanation: Standard setup for the reveal, effective but not particularly original

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: I need someone who's a combination of muscle and single-digit IQ.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Hey!

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: You're perfect.

jokes.text: I think he means you, Tony. Get out of here.
jokes.setup: Everyone realizes Louie means Tony as the muscle with single-digit IQ
jokes.punchline: Tony's indignant 'Get out of here' reaction to the insult
jokes.explanation: Standard reaction beat - Tony's predictable response to the obvious insult

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Get out of here!

jokes.text: I think it sounds like fun. That's my boy.
jokes.setup: Everyone expects Tony to reject Louie's apocalypse plan after the insults
jokes.punchline: Tony thinks it sounds fun, delighting Louie
jokes.explanation: Good character moment - Tony's simple nature makes him miss the insults and focus on adventure

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: You know what, Louie? That actually sounds like fun.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: It does?

jokes.text: It's got pictures in there.
jokes.setup: Louie gives Tony a survival pamphlet to read
jokes.punchline: Mentions it has pictures, implying Tony can't read well
jokes.explanation: Standard condescending joke - efficient but predictable

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: It's got pictures.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Pictures?

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Yeah, pictures. You know, little drawings?

jokes.text: Yeah. Yeah. Oh, yeah? Is that what you think? Yeah?! Yeah. Yeah, yeah! You do think that? Yeah. Yeah, well, I'm going to show you, Reiger.
jokes.setup: Alex challenges Louie's survival group effectiveness
jokes.punchline: Increasingly agitated 'Yeah?!' exchange showing Louie getting worked up
jokes.explanation: Physical comedy beat showing Louie's escalating frustration - works but not economical

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Yeah?

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Yeah!

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Yeah?

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Yeah!

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Yeah?

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: YEAH!

jokes.text: It's an atomic blast that starts an earthquake, setting off civil rioting.
jokes.setup: Group debates which disaster to simulate
jokes.punchline: Louie combines all disasters into one ridiculous scenario
jokes.explanation: Good escalation showing Louie's all-or-nothing mentality

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Alright, listen up. We're gonna simulate a disaster. But not just one disaster. We're gonna do 'em all at once.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Picture this: A earthquake hits, right? But at the same time, a tornado touches down. And there's a flood. And a fire. All happening in this garage.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Plus, a swarm of locusts. And the power goes out. And somehow, a shark swims through here.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Now that's a disaster worth simulatin'.

jokes.text: Are we talking about a, a little adorable muppet?
jokes.setup: Alex describes a starving little girl who might beg for food
jokes.punchline: Louie's inappropriate 'adorable muppet' description shows his disconnection from reality
jokes.explanation: Great character moment - Louie reduces human suffering to cute imagery

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: An adorable little muppet begging for food? That's not a tragedy, that's a dinner and a show!

jokes.text: golden ringlets, just a hint of a lisp?
jokes.setup: Louie continues describing the hypothetical starving child
jokes.punchline: Adds unnecessarily specific cute details like 'golden ringlets' and 'hint of a lisp'
jokes.explanation: Continuation of the absurd romanticization - shows Louie's detachment from reality

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Golden ringlets, a hint of a lisp...

jokes.text: She's buzzard bait.
jokes.setup: Alex asks what Louie would do about the starving little girl
jokes.punchline: Callous 'buzzard bait' dismissal after the cute description
jokes.explanation: Perfect dark comedy - the contrast between cute imagery and callous conclusion is genuinely shocking and perfectly in character

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Starving little girl? I'd tell her to get a job.

jokes.text: You enlist in the Army and you spend a year of your life in Vietnam.
jokes.setup: Someone asks how to get Tony's army outfit
jokes.punchline: Tony matter-of-factly describes the traumatic process of getting authentic military gear
jokes.explanation: Dark humor delivered with Tony's typical straightforward manner - undercuts frivolous question with serious reality

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: You go down to the Army surplus store. You try on the uniform. You look in the mirror. You see yourself as you were twenty years ago. You start sweating. Your hands shake. You remember things you spent two decades trying to forget. You buy the uniform and leave without saying a word to the cashier.

jokes.text: Who played Lumpy in Leave It to Beaver?
jokes.setup: Louie asks if there are any questions about the survival drill
jokes.punchline: Alex asks completely irrelevant TV trivia question
jokes.explanation: Good deflation of Louie's seriousness with perfectly timed irrelevant question

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Any questions about the survival drill?

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Yeah, I got one. On Gilligan's Island, what was the name of the boat?

jokes.text: Good-bye to the world as we... No, no, no... figure of speech.
jokes.setup: Louie dramatically tells others to say goodbye to the world
jokes.punchline: Catches himself getting too dramatic and backpedals with 'figure of speech'
jokes.explanation: Good self-awareness moment showing Louie catching himself being too dramatic

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Say goodbye to the world. Say goodbye to your loved ones. This is it. The end is near.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Figure of speech.

jokes.text: I just had a terrifying thought. If the world were to end tomorrow, that's what's left of the human race.
jokes.setup: Watching Louie's survival group prepare
jokes.punchline: Realizes this incompetent group would be humanity's survivors
jokes.explanation: Excellent meta-observation about the absurdity of the survival premise

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Wait a minute... if this is humanity's last hope, we're in real trouble.

jokes.text: These here have the nutritional equivalent of an entire egg breakfast.
jokes.setup: Louie serves unappetizing survival food
jokes.punchline: Oversells terrible legumes with grandiose nutritional claims
jokes.explanation: Standard joke about survival food being unappetizing despite health claims

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: These are the finest legumes money can buy. High in protein, low in fat, packed with essential vitamins and minerals.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: One bowl of these beans and you'll have the energy of a Olympic athlete, the mental clarity of a genius, and the complexion of a movie star.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: I'm telling ya, these legumes are better than any multivitamin on the market. You eat these every day, you'll live to be a hundred.

jokes.text: It's three to one, Louie.
jokes.setup: Group votes for ice cream over legumes
jokes.punchline: Tony counts Iggy's illegal vote, showing his poor math and rule-following
jokes.explanation: Shows Tony's literal-mindedness in counting votes from someone who shouldn't be there

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Alright, let's count the votes. Ice cream... ice cream... ice cream... and Iggy votes for legumes.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: That's four votes for ice cream, one for legumes. Ice cream wins!

dialogue.speaker: Iggy
dialogue.line: Wait, you counted my vote?

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Sure, why not?

jokes.text: Oh, I've been living in here for three days. Eating this great food, listening to the short wave, reading all those books, and sleeping in these crazy sleeping bags.
jokes.setup: Jim appears unexpectedly in the survival bunker
jokes.punchline: Casually reveals he's been secretly living there for days, treating it like a hotel
jokes.explanation: Perfect Jim moment - his oblivious enjoyment of the situation shows classic character traits

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Casually reveals he's been secretly living there for days, treating it like a hotel

jokes.text: I haven't got the faculty of Harvard in here as it is.
jokes.setup: Louie complains about Jim's lack of intelligence
jokes.punchline: Sarcastically refers to his current group as 'faculty of Harvard'
jokes.explanation: Good self-aware sarcasm about the intellectual level of his chosen survival team

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Jim's so dumb, he makes a rock look like a genius.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: You know what? I got news for you people. We're the faculty of Harvard.

jokes.text: Hey, he could have my legumes.
jokes.setup: Louie insists Jim is threatening their food supply
jokes.punchline: Jeff offers to give Jim the unappetizing legumes nobody wanted
jokes.explanation: Simple callback to the food complaints with practical solution

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: I'll give Jim the lentils.

jokes.text: there's nothing out there; no air, no water, no food, just total devastation.
jokes.setup: Louie tries to convince Tony to throw Jim out
jokes.punchline: Increasingly dramatic description of the outside world to justify cruelty
jokes.explanation: Shows Louie's increasing desperation as his authority is challenged

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Tony, you gotta throw Jim out of here. It's a jungle out there!

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: What are you talking about, Louie?

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: It's not just a jungle, it's a concrete jungle. There are predators on every corner. Muggers, thieves, people who double-park!

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: So you think Jim should stay here?

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Stay here? The man can't even cross the street without dodging buses, cabs, and yellow cabs driven by lunatics! Out there, it's madness, Tony. Pure madness. The weak don't survive. Only the strong, the cunning, the ruthless.

jokes.text: I can't do it, Louie.
jokes.setup: Louie demands Tony throw out Jim despite the friendship
jokes.punchline: Tony's simple refusal shows his basic decency trumps Louie's authority
jokes.explanation: Important character moment where Tony's humanity overcomes his simplicity

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: You're gonna have to fire Jim. I don't care if he's your best friend.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: No, Louie. I'm not doing it.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: You're telling me no? Me?

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: That's right. Jim stays.

jokes.text: A hug?
jokes.setup: Jim is about to be thrown out into 'devastation' and asks for one last thing
jokes.punchline: Instead of food or supplies, he asks for a hug
jokes.explanation: Perfect Jim moment - his innocent emotional needs undercut the entire dark premise with pure humanity

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Before you throw me out into that devastation, could I ask for one last thing?

dialogue.speaker: Other Character
dialogue.line: What is it?

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: A hug.

jokes.text: See, Louie? It's no fun pretending everybody's dead.
jokes.setup: Tony watches the emotional impact of the 'drill'
jokes.punchline: Simple observation that cuts to the heart of why the exercise fails
jokes.explanation: Tony's simple wisdom punctures the entire premise - sometimes the obvious truth is the funniest

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: You know what the problem is with this drill? Nobody's actually afraid of a fire. They're all just afraid of missing their lunch break.

jokes.text: Well, an 11-minute Armageddon. Sort of an 'Armageddonette.'
jokes.setup: Louie's survival drill ends quickly in failure
jokes.punchline: Alex coins 'Armageddonette' for the brief apocalypse simulation
jokes.explanation: Genuinely clever wordplay that perfectly captures the scale of the failure - highly quotable

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Well, that was Armageddonette.

jokes.text: I was done in by a soft heart.
jokes.setup: Louie reflects on why his survival plan failed
jokes.punchline: Admits his downfall was compassion - something he'd never normally claim to have
jokes.explanation: Good character irony - Louie discovers he has feelings he didn't know he possessed

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: You know what my problem was? I had a survival plan, it was foolproof. But then I got compassionate. Can you believe that? Me, compassionate. That's what did me in.

jokes.text: When the end comes, I'll be nothing but a charcoal briquette.
jokes.setup: Louie laments his failure to be ruthless
jokes.punchline: Vivid image of himself as a 'charcoal briquette' after nuclear war
jokes.explanation: Another vivid Louie image of post-apocalyptic transformation, maintaining his creative doom metaphors

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: I've tried to be tough, I've tried to be ruthless, but I just can't do it. You know what I'd be after a nuclear war? A charcoal briquette.

jokes.text: Did you say something, Reiger? Ah, no, no, no, no, I'm sorry, I wasn't listening.
jokes.setup: Alex delivering a heartfelt speech about hope and compassion
jokes.punchline: Louie claiming he wasn't listening but then contradicting himself
jokes.explanation: Perfect deflating of the earnest moment. Louie's defensive denial while proving he heard everything is well-crafted.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: You know, Louie, I've been thinking about what really matters in this world. It's not money or success. It's hope, it's compassion, it's treating people with dignity and respect.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Yeah, yeah, I wasn't listening. I was thinking about lunch.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: You weren't listening?

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Not a word. But whatever you said, it was beautiful. Really moved me.

jokes.text: You sweet, little tender rascal, you.
jokes.setup: Alex gives thoughtful speech about hope and compassion
jokes.punchline: Louie's inappropriately affectionate response to Alex's wisdom
jokes.explanation: Classic Louie awkwardness - his emotional vulnerability comes out as inappropriate affection

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: That was beautiful, Alex. You know, you're alright for a guy who drives a cab.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Come here, you little weasel, and give me a hug!

tmdb_overview: Louie becomes scared of nuclear war after hearing someone talk on Donahue, and he sets up a bomb shelter in the garage. He schedules a weekend drill with Jeff and Tony in tow, but finds he's too ""soft"" to survive if war really broke out, since he is unwilling to throw Jim (who has stowed away in a radiation suit) out of the shelter.
insight: Absurdist charm carries 47 jokes in 20 minutes despite middling craft scores.
